factors.name: US Dollar weakness (DXY)
factors.summary: USD retreating sharply across multiple pairs (EUR/USD toward 1.1645, GBP/USD toward 1.3500, AUD/USD at 0.7167) on geopolitical de-escalation optimism. Weaker USD is single largest structural bullish driver for gold.

factors.name: Real yields (10y TIPS)
factors.summary: No explicit TIPS data in news; however, expectations for potential Hormuz reopening and oil price collapse ($90–$95 WTI/Brent) suggest inflation risks subsiding. Real yields likely stable to slightly lower, mildly supportive.

factors.name: Oil prices / inflation expectations
factors.summary: Crude oil fell 6–7% on Monday (WTI near $90/bbl, down from >$100) on hopes of Hormuz reopening and 30-day ceasefire extension. Lower energy inflation reduces Fed inflation-fight urgency, easing pressure on real rates.

factors.name: Geopolitical de-escalation (US-Iran)
factors.summary: Trump administration and Iran negotiating ceasefire extension (60 days), Strait reopening in ~30 days, and uranium enrichment compromise. Risk-off pressure lifted; risk-on flows favor commodities and weaken safe-haven USD.

factors.name: Risk sentiment (equities, commodities, carry)
factors.summary: Equity futures surging to all-time highs on Hormuz peace optimism. AUD/USD, commodity-linked currencies rallying. Risk-on environment typically benefits gold via weaker USD more than it hurts gold via safe-haven flows in this scenario.

factors.name: Fed policy / rate expectations
factors.summary: No new FOMC decision in window; however, Bloomberg reports Treasury curve flashing 'higher-for-longer' warning under new Fed chair Warsh. Suggests markets pricing in sustained higher rates, which would normally pressure gold, but is being overwhelmed by USD weakness.

factors.name: Recent gold price action
factors.summary: Gold up ~1.3–1.4% on Monday alone, bouncing from $4,519 lows to $4,570+. Move is fresh but now 3+ hours in; no major intraday pullback yet reported.

factors.name: Underlying geopolitical risk (Israel-Lebanon, Russia-Ukraine)
factors.summary: Despite US-Iran optimism, Israel intensifying strikes on Hezbollah and Lebanon, Russia threatening Kyiv. These cross-currents provide mild safe-haven bid but are being subordinated to Hormuz deal narrative.

factors.name: Gold ETF flows / institutional positioning
factors.summary: No GLD/IAU flow data in news window; positioning unknown. However, intra-day rally suggests some momentum buying into weakness.

factors.name: Competing safe havens
factors.summary: USD weakness implies yen also weakening (USD/JPY retreating from 159.00); JPY strength from intervention fears might compete for safe-haven flows, but net effect is dollar weakness = gold bullish.

screening.narrative: Gold rallied 1.3–1.4% on Monday as hopes for a US-Iran ceasefire extension and Strait of Hormuz reopening (~30 days) collapsed oil prices 6–7% and sent the US Dollar sharply lower across all major pairs. The geopolitical de-escalation narrative is the dominant driver: lower oil implies lower inflation expectations, reducing Fed pressure, while weaker USD mechanically lifts gold. Risk-on sentiment (equities at all-time highs) is reinforcing commodity strength over safe-haven demand. However, the move is now in Phase 2 (underway ~3+ hours), and no pullback has occurred; confidence is capped by the lack of confirmed retest or reversal structure.
catalysts_ahead: US Consumer Confidence (CB) due Tuesday (May 26) — economic strength reading could stabilize USD
Earnings releases Tuesday (Elbit Systems, Zscaler, Semtech) — limited macro impact
Continued US-Iran negotiations (next 24–72h) — any negative headlines would reverse move sharply
Israel-Lebanon escalation risk — if widens, could trigger risk-off and safe-haven gold bid
Fed speakers (Powell, Waller, Williams) — any hawkish surprise could crush gold rally
Actual Hormuz mine-clearing progress / 30-day timeline confirmation — key for oil/inflation narrative
screening.risk_warning: The dominant risk is a reversal in the US-Iran narrative. If negotiations stall, fail, or geopolitical violence escalates (Israel-Lebanon, Houthis, etc.), oil will spike back above $100, inflation expectations reset higher, USD will reverse and strengthen, and gold will face sharp downside. Additionally, any hawkish Fed commentary (Warsh 'higher-for-longer' signals) or surprise inflation print would invalidate the lower-real-yields tailwind. Gold is also entering overbought short-term (up 1.4% in one session on thin volume); late-cycle phases often see quick pullbacks before resuming higher.
screening.entry_guidance: Move is in progress and not yet 4+ hours extended; consider scale-in on a pullback to $4,550–$4,560 (retest of 50% fib of the move) rather than chasing $4,575 resistance. If $4,519 support is broken on escalation news, the entire thesis fails and gold could crater to $4,450–$4,480.
